channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.mmorpg.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.mmorpg.com

item.title: Windrose Celebrates 1 Million Copies Sold In Less Than One Week
item.content:encoded:
Early Access pirate survival game, Windrose, has sold through 1 million copies in just six days, according to developer Kraken Express. 

The survival game launched on Steam last week and has become an instant hit, as Kraken Express has announced it has sold over 1 million copies on Steam since last Tuesday. The pirate survival game sees players explore the archipelago of Caribbean-influenced islands, building their pirate base, smuggling supplies with various factions, and sailing the high seas, taking the fight to the infamous Blackbeard and his pirate raiders. 

Via today's announcement:

"Ahoy, captains! We have just plundered another milestone – 1,000,000 copies, and “we” means “you” – a crew of fine brave captains sailing the seas of Windrose. Moreover, today Windrose has assembled over 200,000 concurrent players! Your support for the game is absolutely astonishing, and your patience while we are working on fixes is truly heart-warming. While we keep working on the game, we read your reviews and comments, watch your videos and streams, and sometimes, honestly, shed a pirate-y tear of happiness, because now when the game is out you share our dream of swashbuckling adventure. And there are boars, too, we know, but whatever does not send you flying into the nearest tree, makes you stronger, right?

Thank you for this, captains. You are our inspiration in this journey, and YARRRRR, that’s going to be an exciting one."

It's proven to be a hit, and I've personally put entirely too much time into it over the last week as I've been working on our review. The mix of casual survival mechanics that remind me of Valheim while providing a pretty compelling pirate fantasy has clicked in a way that I didn't think would be possible from the start. 

The team isn't just celebrating the 1 million copy milestone, though, as the early access title also saw a peak concurrent player count on Steam of 222K players yesterday. This is despite the game struggling with connection issues on hosted servers that caused issues early on. 

Yesterday, the Kraken Express team released a hotfix to address this, with direct IP connections added to the connection browser, as well as fixes targeted at save backups, especially if you're playing on multiple devices (like I am). You can check out the details on the Steam page, and keep an eye out for our review-in-progress this week.  



https://www.mmorpg.com/news/windrose-celebrates-1-million-copies-sold-in-less-than-one-week-2000137856
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Interview: Double Fine's Kiln Feels Surprisingly Like A MOBA
item.content:encoded:
Double Fine Productions has always been a studio creating odd games. It’s best known for Psychonauts, an excellent platformer that’s surprisingly one of the studio’s more straightforward games. Since then, Double Fine has created some weirdly charming adventures like Costume Quest, Massive Chalice, and Rad. Last year’s Keeper was the studio’s most abstract game yet, following a walking lighthouse and a bird as they travel through a surrealist post-apocalyptic environment. It also featured no spoken dialogue, instead using visual indicators to convey emotion and gameplay.

Double Fine’s upcoming project, Kiln, is continuing the studio’s reputation, as it’s a multiplayer brawler involving…pottery? We briefly played 30 minutes of Kiln and spoke with director Derek Brand about how the idea came to be and the game’s long development cycle.

Kiln started as a project under Double Fine’s Amnesia Fortnight 2017 internal game jam, and studio head Tim Schafer ultimately chose for it to go under quiet development. However, it’s been nine years since then, so why did it take so long? The answer is simple: Brand and his team were pulled into other projects while working on Kiln. Early development started on the game in 2019 after he had finished his work on Rad. Then, of course, COVID-19 hits in 2020.

“That was a little weird for a while, and then we really had to finish Psychonauts 2. So my team got pulled in to help finish it,” he explains. “When that was done and wrapped up, then we went back to Kiln. And that's also when we broke off and worked on Keeper.” It wasn’t until after Psychonauts 2 was done that the developers could start ramping up on other projects.

Kiln also originally had a slightly darker atmosphere with goofy-looking spirits, and Brand’s team always wanted a more playful tone. Eventually, they decided on the concept of pottery because of its broad appeal. You can see the remnants of the spirit concept while you’re waiting in Kiln’s hub area. You float around as a small spirit inhabiting big pieces of pottery in preparation for battle. Brand wanted to create a pottery power fantasy and welcome those who wouldn’t normally play games.

“We think there's a lot of potential for people who just like making pots,” Brand says. The team also added different cosmetic attachments to give players a balance on whether they want to decorate their pots. He continues, ”There are some very light-hearted and cute things. Then there's also some heavy metal spikes and more metal materials. So you can really go either way, if you want to.”

Before my hands-on time with Kiln, I took a one-hour pottery session, and I discovered how much force and precision I needed to craft a pot that looked even. You need to push the clay around, splash water on it so it doesn’t dry, and drive your elbows into your hips to stabilize yourself. It’s quite a lot of work! In Kiln, the process to actually create the pot is much easier. You just simply need to use the control sticks to push in the direction you want to mold your creation. Want a taller flask? Just move your control stick upwards.

Early on in development, parts of Kiln felt more like a pottery simulator, but players weren’t jiving with those aspects. “We had some really interesting tech that felt like you had to push the clay around, and it kind of fought you a little bit, and had to get really good over time to make anything interesting out of it,” Brand explains. “And ultimately, it was just really punishing, and people bounced off of that.”

As a result, the team stripped away many of the more complex and difficult sculpting mechanics, and started providing more assists. “We wanted to make sure that if you just held and moved the control stick, that you could make a cool shape,” he says. “So we really tried to find areas where we could reintroduce some of the more realistic aspects of the clay, but do that in a way that was more welcoming and more approachable.”

Depending on how you sculpt your clay and the size of it, your pot can fit into three typical size archetypes: small, medium, and large. They all come with different abilities and attacks, such as swinging a sword or running into enemies. Kiln’s primary multiplayer mode is called “Quench,” where teams of 4vs4 must collect water to put out the kiln fire on the opponent’s side of the field.

While Double Fine defines Kiln as a brawler, I was surprised at how much the matches felt like playing a MOBA. The arena was filled with twists and turns. Navigating them was like defending a lane like in League of Legends. I also wiped out other players while they were trying to transport water, and vice versa. The respawn times allowed players on both sides to regroup and strategize.

Brand notes that the team has actually heard the MOBA comparisons, since the objective of Quench is to attack the enemy base.“We didn't necessarily look at MOBAs, but I think there's a lot of validity in that comparison,” he explains. “Really, we see [Quench] as this is our main game mode right now. We want to expand their game modes, but this is just one game mode that this one feels particularly similar to MOBA objectives.” However, Kiln is still a brawler at heart, and there are plans for future game modes with a larger variety of objectives.

Kiln will be on Xbox Game Pass when it launches on April 23 for PC, PS5, and Xbox Series X|S. Brand wants the game to be a comfortable and accessible experience that you don’t have to sink massive amounts of time into, unlike other online games that demand that you act like it’s a part-time job.

“I see a game that's really great for Game Pass, and we want a bunch of players to play it. It's something you can dive into and not commit a ton of time to. I just want to dive in and make some pottery, fight, and then collect a few cool new things that I can put on my future pots,” he explains. “I think there’s a place for that kind of game.”




https://www.mmorpg.com/interviews/interview-double-fines-kiln-feels-surprisingly-like-a-moba-2000137852
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: The Special Ruleset Vengeance Campaign is Back Today in The Elder Scrolls Online
item.content:encoded:
Things have been pretty busy in and around The Elder Scrolls Online in recent weeks. for the PvPers, the ESO team has got you, with a new Vengeance campaign starting today, with some important changes.

The Vengeance campaign is a special ruleset PvP mode in Cyrodiil that levels the playing field by limiting everyone eligible (levels 10 to 50) to build templates and normalized stats. When you enter, your character will change to match a certain build template for their class, and you'll get a backpack of supplies. The only things you take with you when the campaign ends are XP, gold, Alliance Points, and Champion Points. (The Champion System is disabled during the campaign.)

This time around, the campaign will run through April 27th and while this campaign is smaller in scope, there are a few changes to be aware of. First, some changes are intended to increase power for DPS players. Now, you can target more enemies using area of effect abilities, going up to six from a cap of three. These tweaks should speed things up and make them more aggressive. It should also impact healing, with more enemies targeted at once, you’lve got to be strategic and active if you’re the one doing the healing.

You can take part in this latest round via the Alliance War menu. More details on this month's campaign and Vengeance overall is over here in the announcement. 



https://www.mmorpg.com/news/the-special-ruleset-vengeance-campaign-is-back-today-in-the-elder-scrolls-online-2000137853
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Minimo Interview: Can You Fit an MMO Into 30 Minutes?
item.content:encoded:
Emilien Lecoffre Updated: Apr 20, 2026 3:47 PM Posted: Apr 20, 2026 2:30 PM ET
Category: Interviews 0

Announced on March 25th and developed by Jon Selin and Mike Hines at their two-person Atlanta studio Low Drag Labs, Minimo describes itself as a "massively cooperative roguelike" - a genre label that doesn't exist yet. The pitch: 200 players drop into a procedurally generated world for 30-minute sessions, working together toward shared objectives that shift with every run. You could almost call it a Mini-MMO, and I find the concept genuinely intriguing given where the genre stands today. 

We've been asking ourselves lately what an MMO even is anymore, and in particular how much persistence matters to that definition. Minimo leans into that question head-on - it fully embraces the erosion of persistence, replacing it with replayability as a core design pillar. Each run is self-contained, and the roguelike structure turns what might feel like a limitation into a feature. 

The timing feels significant, too. We're reaching what feels like the end of an era for AAA MMORPGs, and with that comes the need for new funding models - which inevitably means new design models, likely built around smaller, leaner scopes. My conviction is that independent developers are well positioned to thrive in this landscape. And from what I've seen of Minimo while researching for this interview, it's a project with real ambition: it aims to revolutionize what an MMO can be, “by making them bite-sized”, and it brings genuinely compelling ideas to the table. We put our questions to Jon and Mike to dig into the details.

MMORPG: Minimo compresses what we could call “the full arc of an MMO” into 30 minutes - leveling, gearing, exploring, boss-killing. Could you walk us through a single run from drop to extraction: what does minute one look like versus minute twenty-five?

Jon Selin, co-founder Low Drag Labs: When a run starts, it feels like the starting gun of a fresh MMO server. Because of the roguelike structure, nobody knows exactly how things will play out - which areas will matter, which encounters will show up, or what builds will emerge. Players are experimenting early: swapping classes based on preference or group needs, forming loose comps, and spreading out to explore and gather resources.

By minute twenty-five, things have usually come together. You’ve got builds and synergies taking shape, much of the map has been uncovered, and the group is pushing into the later areas. That’s when the focus shifts to execution - taking on endgame bosses, sometimes ones you’ve never seen before, chasing unlocks like new classes, or trying to push a deeper run and beat your high score on the leaderboard.

You’ve announced 50 classes, including non-combat roles like chef, fisherman, and flower-picker. In a 30-minute run where every second counts, what stops a group of 200 from simply ignoring the non-combat masks and brute-forcing content with 200 Damage-dealers?

JS: You could do that, but it’s very sub-optimal.

The game is designed so that strong runs rely on a mix of roles. On the combat side, you need things like tanking, healing, different damage profiles, and buffs/debuffs to handle tougher encounters efficiently. But that extends beyond combat as well - activities like fishing, cooking, and mining are how you generate resources, unlock buffs, and enable stronger builds. Those systems have their own layers of optimization too. Different gathering roles, crafting, and even transporting resources all contribute to the overall power of the group.

The intent is that players naturally self-organize around those needs. Some focus on combat, others on enabling it, and the strongest runs come from how those pieces come together. And because each run is procedural - with different maps, encounters, and upgrade paths - the “right” composition shifts every time.

The democratic upgrade system has 200 players voting on shared power-ups. How does the voting actually work in practice? Is it majority rules, weighted by contribution, or something else? And what happens to the player who wanted a different build direction?

JS: At its core, this is a social game, and part of the gameplay is navigating that friction - convincing others, organizing, and making decisions under time pressure. Forming groups, building influence, and aligning people around a direction is as much a skill as combat or optimization.

Right now, it’s a simple majority vote. But we’re exploring alternatives,  for example, a weighted roll system has come up as an interesting idea, and it’s something we’d like to test.

You won’t always get the outcome you want, and that’s intentional. A big part of the experience is adapting - to the procedural world, to the upgrades you’re offered, and to the decisions the group makes. That uncertainty is what keeps each run feeling fresh, and the social layer plays a huge role in that.

You’ve built guild systems with customizable social spaces and leaderboards, but runs last 30 minutes and rosters can reach 200 strangers. How do you expect social bonds to form?

JS: A core part of MMOs is simply existing in a world with other players - those unscripted moments where you run into someone struggling with a miniboss, jump in to help, revive a downed player, or assist in getting resources back to base.

We’re designing Minimo around strong cooperative dependency. Players need to communicate and work together to solve problems, with inspiration from games like Overcooked and Helldivers, but at MMO scale.

When you create those meaningful, moment-to-moment interactions, social bonds form naturally. That’s what leads to players grouping up again, adding each other, and eventually joining guilds or community spaces outside the run.

Jon, you’ve participated in WoW world-first races and Elden Ring speedruns. Is Minimo designed to reward that kind of hardcore optimization?

JS: 100%. Getting the 2nd NA Ragnaros and wanting to democratize those moments was a big inspiration - those peak MMO experiences and organization around them usually only a tiny fraction of players ever get to see. They’re some of the most memorable moments in gaming, but they’re buried deep behind time, coordination, and commitment. We’re

With Minimo, we’re trying to bring that feeling to the surface. If you’re a hardcore player, there’s a lot to optimize - competing on leaderboards for boss kills, damage output, or fastest run times, and organizing large groups to push for world-record runs.

The April playtest is your first large-scale public test. What is the single most important question you need this playtest to answer? And what result would make you rethink a core design decision?

JS: We’re trying to create a new kind of game, and that’s a journey in stages. The first step was validating the idea - does this resonate at all? The overwhelming response to the trailer gave us a lot of confidence that we’re onto something. Now the next step is putting it in players’ hands and seeing how it actually plays.

This playtest marks the start of the next chapter: running structured playtests in waves, where players can give feedback and help shape the direction of the game. We’re looking to understand what’s resonating, where players are getting stuck, and what needs improvement or expansion.

Even with 25+ years in the industry, it’s still nerve-wracking and super exciting to run our first public playtest. There’s a lot for us to learn - even at a fundamental level - about how to run a successful playtest on our infrastructure.

Minimo has no announced business model yet. Given your decade in free-to-play mobile at Pocket Gems, Jon, how are you thinking about monetization ; and how do you reassure the MMO audience, which has been burned repeatedly by aggressive cash shops, that Minimo won’t follow that path?

JS: It’s too early to share exact details, but I’m happy to talk about the principles that will guide it.

Progression in Minimo is primarily horizontal - you’re unlocking new classes, cosmetics, and playstyles, not climbing a power curve. The goal is that players can jump in together regardless of time invested, and that every class - including starter classes - has a role at the highest level of content. A skilled new player should still be able to contribute meaningfully.

On a personal level, a big reason I went indie was to get away from the kind of design pressure where monetization starts driving every decision and takes over the entire development process. We’ll share specifics when we’re ready, but the philosophy is simple: gameplay comes first, progression stays fair, and we avoid creating power gaps where money determines success.

Your trademark filing covers PC, consoles, and mobile devices. Is Minimo being designed as a PC-first game with potential ports, or are you building for all platforms simultaneously? How does that choice affect the complexity of what you can put on screen with 200 players?

JS: Minimo is fundamentally a social game - the whole point is being able to invite your friends to jump in and play with you. And social games are only as strong as the number of people who can actually join. That’s why we’re designing with cross-platform accessibility in mind from the start. The goal is that you can pick up whatever device you have - iPad, Chromebook, PC - and get into a run together.

Right now, internally, we’re testing across PC, Mac, and WebGL, along with controller support. Mobile is next, and consoles will follow.

That approach has a big impact on how we build the game. Supporting 200 players means we have to be extremely disciplined about performance and readability. Everything needs to be clear, efficient, and scalable across a wide range of hardware, and our tech stack decisions have been shaped around that from day one.

Jon, your career path goes MUDs → Dreamlords at Lockpick → World of Darkness at CCP → a decade of mobile at Pocket Gems. Mike, you’ve shipped art at Perpetual, Epic, and Oculus. What specifically did each of those experiences teach you that you’re applying directly to Minimo’s design?

JS: I’ve always been drawn to creating new types of experiences - whether that’s working on new platforms, new genres, or finding different ways for players to interact.

The throughline across everything I’ve worked on is social gameplay. Real collaboration, where players rely on each other and form relationships through shared experiences. That shows up in a lot of ways: giving players different roles and playstyles, making sure everyone can contribute regardless of experience level, and creating systems where groups become stronger than the sum of their parts.

There’s also a strong focus on exploration and discovery - unlocking new abilities, experimenting with different builds, and finding your own path through the game. And just as importantly, building something that’s endlessly replayable, where each session feels like its own unique journey.

Some people are surprised by aspects of Minimo given my background with darker, more PvP-focused games, but those were driven by the same core ideas. This is just a different expression of them - with cooperation and large-scale social play front and center.

Mike Hines, co-founder Low Drag Labs: A big part of my work has always been figuring out how to build experiences that scale, both technically and visually.

On the art side, that means leaning into stylization and a strong visual language. When you have a lot happening on screen, like 200 players at once, clarity becomes essential. Every class, ability, and threat needs to be instantly readable so players can make quick decisions. On the technical side, it means building systems that can support that scale across a wide range of platforms. That intersection of gameplay needs and technical constraints is a big part of what shaped Minimo’s look.

I’ve also always been focused on creating worlds that feel varied and alive. Different biomes, encounters, and environmental storytelling help make each run feel distinct, even within a structured session.

And a big part of the work has been building tools and pipelines that let a small team move quickly. For a game like Minimo, where replayability and variation are core, being able to generate, tune, and iterate on content efficiently is just as important as the content itself. All of that comes together in Minimo. It has to stay readable, performant, and scalable.

A Smaller Scale for a Bigger Future?

One thing that stands out from this conversation is how aware Jon and Mike are of the technical constraints surrounding their project. For an online game - especially one targeting 200 concurrent players - that's essential, and the seriousness with which they approach it is reassuring. Another takeaway, one that I personally find less exciting but understand the reasoning behind, is the cross-platform ambition, and particularly the push toward mobile. 

This echoes what other veteran developers have been saying lately. Greg Street, Rich Vogel, and Scott Hartsman, in a WCCFTech interview from a few months ago reflecting on the state of the MMO genre after the wave of high-profile cancellations in late 2025 and early 2026, all made a similar case: the future calls for humbler, more flexible game designs - projects that can grow over time, scale sustainably, and, crucially, actually get funded. In short, making MMO-scale projects more accessible and more reasonable, given the climate surrounding AAA MMORPGs today. 

That funding reality naturally extends to platform availability: reaching as many potential players as possible may not be just a growth strategy, but a survival one. As a longtime PC MMORPG player, I can't say this thrills me - cross-platform development inevitably means technical compromise - and I suspect many readers with a similar profile feel the same way. But given the crisis the genre is going through, it's a bet that makes sense (though only time will tell if it proves a necessity for the genre).

On the monetization front, while Jon didn't share specifics, the philosophy he outlined is encouraging. His emphasis on horizontal progression - where unlocking new classes and playstyles takes priority over climbing a power curve - suggests a genuine commitment to fairness. Reading their answers, I came away with the impression that their goal is to build a game that is not only technically and financially viable, but equitable for every type of player. I'm eager to see how it plays out. I believe we're at a turning point for the MMORPG genre, and projects like Minimo will play a central role in shaping what we consider an MMO - or a mini-MMO - tomorrow. The game's first public playtest is scheduled for April, and if you're as intrigued as I am, you can sign up for access.

Emilien Lecoffre

Emilien 'Nephistos' has been immersed in MMOs since his early teens on Dofus. Over the past years, he also has been sharing his insights on JeuxOnline, a major French-language MMO sites. While he keeps a keen eye on all market offerings, his true passion lies in RvR and mass PvP.



https://www.mmorpg.com/interviews/minimo-interview-can-you-fit-an-mmo-into-30-minutes-2000137851
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Blue Protocol: Star Resonance Confirms Next Month's Mystery Collaboration
item.content:encoded:
Blue Protocol: Star Resonance has revealed its third of three promised anime collaborations. Last month, the team announced collaborations with Shangri-La Frontier ending this week) and Fairy-Tail, but left the third collaboration a mystery.

The third collaboration is with Monogatari Series and is coming in May, following the Fairy-Tail collab. Monogatari features a high school student named Koyomi Araragi, who has been cured of vampirism. Now that he’s human again, he decides to try and help others who have been affected by oddities, usually supernatural in origin, like ghosts and spirits. 

An anime adaptation, was based on a series of light novels, and began airing back in 2009 in Japan. While there’s no info just yet about what this collaboration with the anime-inspired MMORPG will entail, the Shangri-La Frontier campaign had special cosmetics and furniture items, as well as other items coming out throughout the month-long event.

Next month, the Fairy Tail collaboration will feature characters from the Wizard Guild as part of the crossover event. After that collab ends, we can expect the Monogatari event. 

Today is April 20th, so with May just around the corner, we should be hearing lots more info and getting details about both of these collaborations starting pretty soon. 



https://www.mmorpg.com/news/blue-protocol-star-resonance-confirms-next-months-mystery-collaboration-2000137850
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
